one_line_takeaway: UMID shows that identity-level membership inference against CLIP/CLAP models can be performed with text-only queries via cross-modal latent inversion, matching or beating shadow-model and paired-biometric-query baselines in accuracy while running in under a second instead of hours, on models trained from scratch for ground-truth evaluation.
key_finding: On CLIP (Table I), UMID using text-only queries (no real image, 'Null') exceeds 90% accuracy on ResNet-50 and ResNet-50x4 in both One-Shot and Many-Shot settings (e.g., ResNet-50 one-shot: UMID-Null Precision .8634/Recall .9821/Accuracy .9172 vs strongest baseline C-WSA Precision .7210/Recall .4530/Accuracy .7125), a reported gain of up to +18 accuracy points and +50 recall points over C-WSA, while running in 0.628-0.815s versus 4.3+ hours for shadow-model MIA and ~2100s for WSA/C-WSA. Adding one real image pushes UMID accuracy to .9528-.9562 on ResNet backbones; on ViT-B/32 the gains are smaller (UMID-Null accuracy .6837-.6947), attributed to ViT's stronger generalization producing weaker text-only signal. On an imbalanced CelebA split (10% members) UMID achieves 0.941 AUROC with 3.2% FPR at 90% TPR. On CLAP (Table II), UMID-Null reaches 91.27%/93.07% accuracy on LibriSpeech one-/many-shot, and with one real audio sample reaches up to 95.24% (many-shot), exceeding the strongest baseline AuditMI (87.91%/88.16% accuracy) by margins up to +7.08 accuracy points; on the harder CommonVoice dataset all baselines degrade to ~74-76% accuracy while UMID-1audio reaches 83.56%/85.69%. Under a differential-privacy embedding-noise defense (epsilon=1.0), UMID accuracy drops by roughly 6-12 points but remains functional (e.g., CLIP ResNet-50x4: 87.42% one-shot / 85.95% many-shot; CLAP LibriSpeech: 87.88%/83.40%; CommonVoice: 75.90%/79.15%), with runtime essentially unchanged (~0.7s). Ablations show accuracy stabilizes once the gibberish reference set reaches l>=50 strings, a single real sample adds 1.5-3.5% accuracy with diminishing returns beyond one sample, a 3-vote threshold is optimal for text-only queries (4-vote when a real sample is added), and performance peaks near n=100 runs / m=1000 iterations for CLIP (n=50 for CLAP) with negligible gains beyond that.
core_contribution: The paper proposes UMID (Unimodal Membership Inference Detector), a membership-inference auditing method for contrastive pretraining models (CLIP for image-text, CLAP for audio-text) that determines whether a person's PII (name/identity) was present in the training set using ONLY a text query, without ever submitting the person's biometric data (face image or voice) to the target model. UMID performs text-guided cross-modal latent inversion: starting from random noise, it optimizes a synthetic modality input (image or audio embedding) via gradient ascent to maximize cosine similarity with the text embedding of the queried identity, repeated over n independent randomized runs. From these n optimized embeddings it extracts two statistics -- similarity (Sn, mean cosine alignment to the text embedding) and variability (D2n, mean squared dispersion of the optimized embeddings across runs). These two features are compared against a reference ('semantic-null') distribution built from synthetic gibberish strings (generated by prompting GPT-3.5-turbo), and a membership decision is produced by majority vote of an ensemble of unsupervised anomaly detectors (LocalOutlierFactor, IsolationForest, OneClassSVM, AutoEncoder) trained on the gibberish-derived features. An optional enhancement incorporates a local (non-submitted) real biometric sample via an external feature extractor (DeepFace) and K-means clustering to add a third vote. The paper also provides a finite-sample theoretical guarantee (Theorem III.1) that member and non-member statistics (Sn, D2n) are separable with high probability, under a stylized geometric model where members concentrate on a training-induced prototype and non-members are isotropic in the latent space.
threat_model: Gray-box auditing setting: the auditor (e.g., a regulator or third-party compliance auditor) has query and gradient access to the frozen target encoders (text encoder phi_text and modality encoder phi_mod, e.g. a released/open-weight CLIP or CLAP model) but has no access to the training pipeline, training hyperparameters, optimizer states, or the private training dataset itself. Critically, the auditor is assumed to be legally/practically barred from submitting real biometric data (faces, voices) of the target individual to the model, even if such data is in the auditor's possession -- motivated by GDPR/HIPAA-style third-party compliance constraints and EU AI Act-style regulatory audits of open-weight models. The attack/audit surface is therefore restricted to text-only queries (a target identity string t) plus white-box gradient access to run the latent-inversion optimization locally. The paper frames this as resolving an 'auditor's dilemma': prior multimodal MIAs (IDIA, WSA, C-WSA) require submitting paired biometric inputs to the target, which itself creates a secondary privacy exposure risk.
limitations: The paper's own text surfaces several caveats rather than presenting them as external critique: (1) all target models are trained from scratch on custom-built datasets specifically to guarantee ground-truth membership labels; the authors explicitly acknowledge this means results 'reflect true algorithmic capability rather than dataset noise' but do not evaluate against real deployed/public foundation models (e.g., OpenAI's released CLIP checkpoints), so generalization to production models with unknown, contaminated, or heavily deduplicated training corpora is untested. (2) The gray-box threat model assumes the auditor has gradient access to the frozen encoders, which is a nontrivial deployment assumption (not all 'open-weight' audit scenarios plausibly grant computable gradients through both encoders). (3) Effectiveness is markedly weaker on ViT-B/32 (67-71% accuracy range) than on ResNet backbones (>90%), attributed only qualitatively to 'stronger generalization' without a deeper mechanistic explanation. (4) The theoretical separation guarantee (Theorem III.1) relies on a stylized geometric model (K unit-norm prototypes, isotropic non-members, margin-gamma-aligned members) that is a simplification of real learned embedding geometry; the paper offers only indirect empirical validation (convergence plots, isotropy check via cosine similarity to random Gaussian vectors) rather than a direct measurement of the model's true prototype structure. (5) Robustness is tested against only two candidate defenses (DP noise and covert-gibberish adaptive querying); no evaluation against, e.g., an actively adversarial defender who has access to and adapts against UMID's specific detection statistics (Sn, D2n) rather than generic noise injection.

datasets_used: CelebA-style set built from FaceScrub + LAION-5B image-text pairs integrated into CC3M backbone (200 identities, 100 member/100 non-member, One-Shot=1 photo/person, Many-Shot=75 photos/person)
CommonVoice18.0-derived set (3,000 speakers: 1,500 members/1,500 non-members, PII narratives augmented by GPT-4o)
quantitative_results: CLIP ResNet-50 one-shot: UMID-Null Precision .8634/Recall .9821/Accuracy .9172 in 0.628s vs best baseline C-WSA .7210/.4530/.7125 in 2141.4s
CLIP ResNet-50 one-shot with 1 image: UMID Precision .9145/Recall .9912/Accuracy .9528 in 0.745s
CLIP ViT-B/32 one-shot: UMID-Null Accuracy .6837 vs C-WSA .6912 (UMID weakest relative margin on ViT backbone)
Imbalanced CelebA (10% members): UMID AUROC 0.941, FPR 3.2% at 90% TPR
CLAP LibriSpeech one-shot: UMID-Null Accuracy .9127 vs best baseline AuditMI .8791 (0.603s vs 13.578s)
CLAP LibriSpeech many-shot with 1 audio: UMID Precision .8921(reported also as 91.63 in a differently-scaled row)/Recall .9868/Accuracy .9354-.9524
CLAP CommonVoice one-shot: UMID-Null Accuracy .8179 vs AuditMI .7436; UMID with 1 audio Accuracy .8356

Differential privacy defense (epsilon=1.0, delta=1e-5): CLIP ResNet-50x4 accuracy drops from .9535/.9562 (no defense) to .8742/.8595 (one-shot/many-shot); CLAP LibriSpeech drops from .9354/.9524 to .8788/.8340; CLAP CommonVoice drops from .8356/.8569 to .7590/.7915
Covert Gibberish (CG) query defense/variant: accuracy reduction of only 1.23%-1.54% across datasets, e.g. CelebA many-shot .9562 (no defense) to .9385 (CG)

Ablations: accuracy stabilizes at gibberish set size l>=50 (marginal gain <0.5% beyond); one real sample adds 1.5-3.5% accuracy (additional samples add <1%); 3-vote threshold optimal text-only, 4-vote with real sample; n=100/m=1000 near-optimal for CLIP, n=50 for CLAP
